channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Pokémon Champions Preview: A Modern Competitive Battler
item.content:encoded:
PAX East brought Nintendo back to the Boston-based expo, and this year, their booth focused on two titles from the Pokémon Company: Pokémon Pokopia and Pokémon Champions. While the former has already been released on Nintendo Switch 2 and focuses exclusively on creating cozy habits for your Pokémon friends, the latter takes the polar opposite approach and creates an arena dedicated to competitive battling.

Since I already own Pokopia, I was excited to go hands-on with a Pokémon Champions preview at PAX East. While it doesn't significantly depart from the core battling mechanics of what Pokémon veterans are familiar with, it does streamline some of the training and grinding that were necessary in the mainline titles.

Check out what sets Pokémon Champions apart and why it could create a benchmark for competitive Pokémon moving forward.

Pokémon Champions Preview: Training Is More Open-Ended

Pokémon Champions takes much of what was randomized in previous titles and gives the player full control over their lineup. Instead of training and leveling up in a traditional manner, players will be able to pump points directly into EV stats, fully customizing that Pokémon's competitive makeup.

You can also set a Stat Alignment, effectively selecting your Pokémon's Nature so that you can optimize their abilities without hours of grinding and happenstance. Plus, you can select any Move that Pokémon is able to learn, making it easier than ever to build the perfect team.

As you earn Victory Points, these can be pumped into your individual Pokémon and get them to the exact specifications you desire. The best part: Pokémon can be loaded in from Pokémon Home, making it easy to bring your favorites into a competitive arena with you!

Once you get to battling, you may find that the mechanics haven't changed considerably from what players are familiar with, but that's not necessarily a bad thing. 

Pokémon Champions Preview: Battling Is Fun But Familiar 

The battling mechanics in Pokémon Champions function predominantly the same as they do in previous titles: select your team, throw them out into the arena, and select your moves in turn-based combat.

Where Pokémon Champions differentiates itself is the style of bring 6, choose 4. So, when you choose to do a Single Battle or Double Battle, you can see the roster that your opponent has to choose from. This can help inform you of which Pokémon you should send out depending on the match types.

However, since you and your opponent are only selecting 4 of the 6 Pokémon, there's a strategic component of guessing who they're going to choose. Which Pokémon are they going to send into battle, and which ones are just there to throw you off?

For my Pokémon Champions preview, I played two double battles against a human opponent. Our TVs were set up back to back, so I wasn't able to see their expressions or which Pokémon they were choosing. When we got into the battle, I was impressed at how smooth and fast-paced everything felt for a turn-based game.

While there was nothing particularly groundbreaking for the battling mechanics, it just felt good to be commanding Pokémon in a huge arena and watching their moves lash out on the big screen. The Nintendo representative standing nearby was explaining how Pokémon Champions was designed from the ground-up to be the go-to competitive game for championships, and I can see why.

With fully customizable stats, movesets, and Pokémon Home compatibility, Pokémon Champions has widespread appeal across the entire fanbase, making competitive Pokémon matches more accessible than ever before. 

Pokémon Champions Preview: Final Thoughts 

While Pokémon Champions felt good as a battler and feels like the logical next step for this level of gameplay, it doesn't reinvent the Pokéwheel. But that's OK – this is a formula that works and has worked for decades. By taking out a lot of the randomness and grinding that was necessary just to get Pokémon to be competitively ranked, Pokémon Champions is here to earn its place at championships around the world.

Pokémon Champions releases on April 8, 2026, as a free-to-start game, with a mobile version coming later this year.

Pokémon Champions was previewed on a Nintendo Switch at PAX East.

Dan is a Boston-based writer who has been with TechRaptor since the end of 2020. He has been working in the online writing, editing and SEO space for nearly… More about Dan



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/pokemon-champions-preview-modern-competitive-battler
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Steam Store Is Getting a Refresh With New Sections and More
item.content:encoded:
Valve has announced a new Steam store refresh that you can try out right now, and it boasts an updated design for the store itself, plus new sections with extra info in them and more.

In a Steam post (appropriately enough) announcing the refresh, Valve says the redesigned version of the Store's landing page is available now as part of the latest Steam beta. If you're using the standard Steam client, you won't be able to see it yet.

You can look forward to an overall refresh to the design of the Steam store, which Valve says "aims to make the store home feel more cohesive". New sections have been introduced, as have "new ways for you to discover and learn about games".

In the Featured & Recommended section, you should now see more info regarding exactly why you're getting those recommendations, and you'll also see a round-up of user reviews for the game on Steam.

Smaller tweaks include discounts and events using larger game artwork, as well as the ability to disable "micro-trailer hovers and animated marketing assets" should you wish to. When many platforms don't allow you to do this, it's nice to see Valve offering customization options.

New sections on the store include a new Wishlist section, which shows you discounts on games from your wishlist at any given time, as well as a "DLC for Your Games" area, where, as you'd expect, you can see discounted DLC for games you own.

There are lots of other little changes and tweaks that should go towards making your Steam experience that bit smoother, so be sure to check out the full announcement post to see what's new.

In other Steam news, the platform is currently being sued by the UK's Performing Right Society over music rights that the Society alleges Valve's store doesn't have. That comes after Steam broke its concurrent user record (yes, again) last month, reaching over 42.3 million users for the first time.

Steam's new visual store refresh is available right now on the Steam beta branch, and you can see how to sign up for that here if you're interested. Valve hasn't announced when the store redesign will be coming to those on the main branch yet.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/steam-store-refresh-2026
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Call of Duty Veteran Jason Blundell Returns With Another New Studio
item.content:encoded:
Call of Duty veteran Jason Blundell, who hit headlines last week thanks to Sony's closure of his previous studio Dark Outlaw Games, has returned with another new development outfit.

As spotted by the folks at KitGuru, Blundell revealed the existence of his new studio during a conversation with YouTuber and fellow ex-Dark Outlaw staffer JCbackfire. The company's name is Magic Fractal, and we don't know very much about it or what it's up to yet.

Presumably, Blundell will announce more in the coming months, but given that his last studio was closed down by Sony before its first project was even announced, any jitteriness on Blundell's part regarding making another big announcement is entirely understandable.

Blundell also revealed a logo during the conversation with JCbackfire, but it's a fairly innocuous affair, sporting just the company's name and some artfully-arranged retro-style square artwork.

During another stream with JCbackfire last week, Blundell confirmed that the game on which he and Dark Outlaw were working was not a live-service game, despite his extensive experience with the Call of Duty franchise.

As such, it's possible that Blundell, JCbackfire, and the other ex-Dark Outlaw staffers will shift all of the work they've done (however much that may be) on that unannounced project towards whatever Magic Fractal's debut game turns out to be.

However, it's worth noting that the state of the industry right now is volatile, to say the least. Plenty of studios started by starry-eyed industry veterans close down without ever releasing a game, so it's probably best to wait until Blundell and Magic Fractal's first game is a little more concrete before getting excited.

It'd be nice to report, in a few months (or even years), that Blundell finally managed to get a game off the ground after leaving Treyarch, so let's hope that's how things go this time around. We'll bring you more as soon as we get it.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/jason-blundell-another-new-studio-call-of-duty
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Garry's Mod Spiritual Successor s&box Launches Later This Month
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher Facepunch Studios has announced that s&box (say it out loud!), its spiritual successor to Garry's Mod, will be launching later this month.

The release date for s&box was quietly revealed as part of the game's latest patch notes on Steam, which include changes like an overhauled vehicle camera, the ability to save and load play sessions in sandbox mode, and more (and the launch date, of course). The game is coming on April 28th.

Facepunch says it "tried to quietly announce" the release date, but "it seems everyone has got excited", so the attempt to slip under the radar failed, evidently. Prior to launch, Facepunch will revoke s&box from "anyone who hasn't played a lot recently, developed games, or bought skins", after which the game will cost $20.

s&box first entered open beta all the way back in 2021, so it's been in the works for quite some time. At that point, Garry Newman, who, as you've probably figured out, was the driving force behind Garry's Mod, described s&box as an attempt at creating a "worthy" sequel to the creativity tool.

The original Garry's Mod uses the first iteration of the Source engine, but s&box is built on Source 2, the engine that's also been used for games like VR tour de force Half-Life: Alyx and updated shooter Counter-Strike 2 (as well as still-under-wraps hero shooter Deadlock).

The Steam page for s&box describes the project as "a game creation platform" in which you can "play, create, and share games, explore community creations, and experience endless sandbox fun" using "powerful modern tools".

Not only can you make games within s&box, but you can also build cosmetic microtransactions into them, allowing you to earn cash from players checking out your game, Roblox-style. The plan is also to let players publish their games on Steam royalty-free, although Facepunch says it's "not quite ready" for this yet.

If you'd like to check it out for yourself, s&box launches exclusively on PC via Steam on April 28th.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/sandbox-garrys-mod-successor-release-date
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
